newton.note: Revised Definitions section (hd5); cross-ref numbers preserved as newton_def

methodology.lens: Common ancient Israelite knowledge of the natural world (pre-modern science). Figures must be supported by how the word is used within the allowed precedent books — not back-propagated from later genres (e.g. no NT readings applied to Genesis).

genre_rules.narrative: Historical narrative (Genesis–Kings, etc.): figures arise from simile, dream reports with in-text interpretation, covenant signs, and poetic speeches embedded in the story. Do not apply prophetic kingdom↔world systems unless the book itself uses them.
genre_rules.prophetic: Prophetic/apocalyptic: kingdom-world analogies (Newton Definitions) apply when genre and context support them; precedent may include earlier prophets and narrative background.
genre_rules.law: Legal texts: figurative language only where the legal discourse itself uses it (e.g. extended metaphor in parenesis), not imported prophetic codes.

scope_bible.note: Cross-biblical scoped senses by genre and book

sense_layers.note: Pan-biblical canon_senses and genre_senses layered above book scope

man_anatomy.note: Man body parts — Scripture-primary; Genesis precedent for narrative members

apocalyptic.description: Apocalyptic visions; may draw on prophetic precedent where genre matches.

prophetic.description: Major and minor prophets collectively; kingdom-world figures when genre supports.

genesis_only.label: Genesis only
genesis_only.description: Sense limited to Genesis; no later book may inform the reading.

torah_to.label: Torah cumulative
torah_to.description: Sense may use the book in question plus any earlier Torah book.

torah.label: Torah
torah.description: Genesis–Deuteronomy: narrative, covenant signs, law, and embedded poetry. Figures arise from in-text interpretation, ritual, and story — not imported prophetic kingdom-codes unless the Torah passage itself uses them.

historical.label: Historical narrative
historical.description: Joshua–Esther: annals, biography, and national story. Literal and dream-interpreted figures; prophetic systems only when a prophet speaks within the narrative.

wisdom.label: Wisdom & Psalms
wisdom.description: Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Songs: poetry, proverb, and reflective discourse. Simile and personification abound; not automatic Newton political code.

prophetic.label: Prophetic corpus
prophetic.description: Major and minor prophets: kingdom–world analogies (Newton Definitions) when martial, political, or apocalyptic context supports them.

apocalyptic.label: Apocalyptic (Daniel)
apocalyptic.description: Daniel's court tales and visions: beasts, horns, kingdoms, and trial narratives. Distinct from generic prophecy though it shares stock images.

gospels.label: Gospels
gospels.description: Matthew–John: Jesus' teaching, miracles, passion, and resurrection. Parables, kingdom sayings, and OT image re-use — read in narrative and christological context, not as automatic export of Newton's HB code.

jesus_parable.label: Jesus' parables
jesus_parable.description: Short kingdom parables in the Synoptics: one image often maps to one moral or eschatological point; in-text explanation governs.

acts.label: Acts
acts.description: Early church history: Spirit, mission, and persecution. OT and prophetic images echo but are re-read in apostolic setting.

pauline.label: Pauline epistles
pauline.description: Romans–Philemon: argument, parenesis, and ecclesiology. Metaphor is theological and pastoral; allusions to HB figures are reinterpreted in Christ.

general_epistle.label: General epistles
general_epistle.description: Hebrews, James, Peter, John, Jude: exhortation and apocalyptic warning. Vivid metaphor with occasional explicit OT typology.

revelation_nt.label: Revelation
revelation_nt.description: Apocalyptic prophecy: seals, trumpets, beasts, and New Jerusalem. Heavy intertext with Ezekiel, Daniel, and the prophets — figures are vision-coded.

conditions.label: Kingdom compared to the frame of heaven and earth
conditions.description: The original of prophetic figurative language: a kingdom is compared to the universe, and parts of one map to like parts of the other.

conditions.label: Earth signifies a people or aggregate of nations
conditions.description: When earth is put for peoples, terrestrial figures apply.

conditions.label: Sea or waters signify a people or aggregate of nations
conditions.description: When sea/waters are put for peoples, aquatic figures apply.

conditions.label: Waters signify riches
conditions.description: Sometimes waters signify riches rather than peoples; sea and rivers must then be interpreted accordingly (Newton, Def. notes on waters).

conditions.label: Tree proportion extended beyond ordinary scale
conditions.description: Although a tree originally signifies an inferior great man, if represented large beyond proportion (reaching earth's ends) it signifies a king (Dan 4).

conditions.label: Mountain proportion extended beyond ordinary scale
conditions.description: Although a mountain originally signifies a city, if so great as to fill the whole earth it signifies the kingdoms subject to that city (Dan 2:35).

conditions.label: Wild beast armies grow into a kingdom
conditions.description: Although wild beasts originally signify armies, armies that prey upon neighbours and grow into a kingdom may be called a beast.

conditions.label: A named beast stands for a whole kingdom
conditions.description: When lion, bear, leopard, dragon, or nameless monster is put for a kingdom, its anatomical parts map to kingdom parts.

conditions.label: Vision — members named on beast or figure
conditions.description: When Daniel, Ezekiel, or Revelation describes a horn, beast, or heavenly figure with anatomical members, read those members from what the vision and its in-text interpretation say — distinct from ordinary narrative body language.

conditions.label: Divine hand, face, arm (theophany & covenant)
conditions.description: The Lord's hand, face, arm, or finger named in Scripture for power, presence, or redemption — read from the citing passage (e.g. Exod 6:6, Isa 51:16), not imported from another book's beast-vision code.

conditions.label: Genesis creation narrative (Gen 1–2:3)
conditions.description: Cosmic ordering account: waters, light, firmament, luminaries, and land are the visible world as an ancient hearer would know it — not a coded kingdom.

conditions.label: Genesis garden narrative (Gen 2–3)
conditions.description: Eden garden discourse: trees, river, serpent, nakedness, and seed enmity within the story's own terms.

conditions.label: Genesis dream report with in-text interpretation
conditions.description: A dream is narrated and interpreted by speakers within Genesis (e.g. Joseph, Pharaoh, Jacob). Figures are limited to what the text explicitly identifies.

conditions.label: Genesis covenant sign
conditions.description: A visible token set in narrative as a memorial of covenant (e.g. bow in cloud).

conditions.label: Genesis covenant ritual vision
conditions.description: Theophanic objects in a covenant-cutting scene (Gen 15), read as signs within that rite.

conditions.label: Genesis tribal blessing poem (Gen 49)
conditions.description: Jacob's deathbed poetry: animal and plant images for tribes, not apocalyptic beasts.

conditions.label: Genesis seed/offspring promise
conditions.description: Similitudes comparing offspring to dust, stars, or sand in number — within covenant promise speeches in Genesis.

conditions.label: Torah — mountain of God / Sinai revelation
conditions.description: Horeb/Sinai and related heights as places where God meets Israel — literal elevated sites with sacred boundary, not city-figures.

conditions.label: Narrative — literal landform
conditions.description: Mountain as a physical height for travel, battle, burial, or settlement unless the passage itself identifies a figure.

conditions.label: Prophetic — mountain as head city
conditions.description: When earth/peoples are in view, mountain often maps to a city — especially the capital (Jerusalem, Babylon, Samaria). Newton Def. 7.

conditions.label: Prophetic — mountain of the house / holy mountain
conditions.description: Zion's temple mount or "mountain of the Lord" — cultic elevation and dwelling of God; overlaps city but sometimes stresses temple specifically (Newton Def. 8).

conditions.label: Prophetic — mountain as obstacle or hostile power
conditions.description: A mountain personified as blocking restoration or destroying the earth (e.g. Zerubbabel vision, Babylon as destroying mountain).

conditions.label: Prophetic — eschatological mountain exalted
conditions.description: The mountain of the Lord's house lifted above hills, nations flowing to it (Isa 2, Mic 4).

conditions.label: Wisdom/Psalms — poetic mountain imagery
conditions.description: Simile, personification, or refuge language in poetry — mountains praising, quaking, or as God's righteousness.

conditions.label: Apocalyptic — mountain filling the earth
conditions.description: Mountain grown to fill the land/world — dominion of a kingdom (Dan 2:35).

conditions.label: Daniel — fiery furnace trial
conditions.description: Nebuchadnezzar's furnace in Daniel 3 — ordeal and deliverance narrative, distinct from Abram's covenant theophany (Gen 15).

conditions.label: Prophetic — waters as peoples
conditions.description: Newton usage when sea/waters signify nations.

conditions.label: Wisdom — creation or theophany idiom
conditions.description: Cosmic or meteorological language in Job/Psalms drawn from storm, wind, and sea as an ancient observer would describe God's power.

conditions.label: Torah — plague or wonder sign
conditions.description: Elemental judgments in Exodus (hail, locusts, fire on mount) as miraculous signs against Egypt or at Sinai — literal phenomena, not political codes.

conditions.label: Prophetic — consuming fire in war
conditions.description: Fire devouring trees, cities, or beasts when men and their works are represented as combustible — Newton Def. 48.

conditions.label: Prophetic — flood as invasion
conditions.description: Overflowing waters or armies pouring over borders — Newton Def. 23 when peoples are in view.

conditions.label: Prophetic — harlotry as covenant betrayal
conditions.description: Sexual infidelity language for Israel's pursuit of other gods (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) — distinct from literal prostitution in narrative.

conditions.label: Narrative — military horse, rider, bow
conditions.description: Cavalry, chariotry, and archery as ordinary warfare technology in historical books unless a prophetic oracle refigures them.

conditions.label: Literal cultus — altar, sacrifice, priesthood
conditions.description: Altar, offering, and sanctuary language in Torah and history as worship practice, not martyrdom-similitude unless prophetic genre shifts.

conditions.label: Wisdom — Sheol / deep pit
conditions.description: Pit, depths, and Sheol in Psalms/Job/Proverbs as death, danger, or the grave — not apocalyptic heresy-spring unless Daniel-like genre.

entries.label: Frame of heaven and earth
entries.sense: The kingdom compared to the whole frame of heaven and earth.
entries.note:
Newton's kingdom↔universe frame is prophetic in genre. Genesis uses heaven and earth as created realities (see scoped senses on those entries), not as a political allegory of a kingdom.
 Newton Def. 1 frames the whole prophetic system: kingdom compared to heaven and earth. Genesis scoped senses on sun, moon, star, heaven, earth treat created realities separately. Apply kingdom↔world only in prophetic/apocalyptic genre.

entries.label: Sun
entries.sense: The supreme magistrate; kingly power.

scoped_senses.sense: In the creation account, the greater light set to rule the day. In Joseph's dream report, Jacob identifies the sun with himself (the father); read only where the narrative supplies that identification — not as a general code for kings.
Sun standing still, shining on land, daybreak in battle — astronomical day-sign, not king, unless a dream report (cf. Genesis scoped sense).

Darkening or smiting the sun: affliction of the supreme magistrate or day of the Lord obscuring kingly light. Malachi's sun of righteousness is healing — still celestial image tied to royal/messianic day.
Sun rising and setting under heaven — vanity and repetition in observable nature; no political code in this book.

entries.label: Moon
entries.sense: The next in dignity after the supreme magistrate.

scoped_senses.sense: In creation, the lesser light to rule the night. In Joseph's dream, Jacob takes the moon for "thy mother" — a figure only within that dream scene.
Moon as night-light and festival marker — worship warnings against moon- cults, not queen or magistrate unless a dream report supplies it (Genesis).
Moon darkened or turned to blood: affliction of the second dignity under the supreme magistrate when cosmic lights signal national collapse.
Moon praised, sworn by, or fair as the moon — creation beauty and love poetry without political code.

entries.label: Star
entries.sense: Princes or inferior kings; later extended to common people (Def 28).

alternate_senses.sense: Common people (when celestial proportions are extended).

scoped_senses.sense: Luminaries in the sky (creation); countless descendants compared to stars in number in Abrahamic promises; eleven stars as Joseph's brothers in his dream — each usage tied to its own speech, not to princes or kings by default.
Stars as heaven's host God names and rules — creation and warning against worshipping them. Countless number simile stays in promise/oracle context.
Stars as princes, host of heaven cast down, or bright ones who turn many to righteousness — political and angelic ranks in vision.

entries.label: Mountain
entries.sense: A city, especially the head city (Jerusalem, Babylon); sometimes a temple.

alternate_senses.sense: Kingdoms of the earth subject to that city (Dan 2:35).

entries.note: Mountain is highly scope-sensitive: literal landform (dominant in Torah/history), sacred meeting-height (Sinai), prophetic city (Newton), temple-mount (Isa 2/Mic 4), obstacle or destroying power (Zech 4/Jer 51), and apocalyptic kingdom (Dan 2).

scoped_senses.sense: Named heights in the story (Ararat, Moriah, Bethel ridges, etc.) — real mountains for sacrifice, altar, burial, and travel. No in-text code for "city" or "temple" in Genesis.

Mountain of God / Horeb / Sinai: the elevated site of revelation and covenant, bounded as holy ground. Still a physical mountain an Israelite could picture, not a metaphor for a city unless the passage names it so (it does not in Torah).

Mountains as terrain for warfare, refuge, altars, and royal/high-place worship. Meaning is primarily geographic unless a later prophetic citation reuses the place (read that book on its own precedent).

Mixed: literal hills (help comes from the mountains), God's holy mountain as Zion, mountains personified (skipping, quaking, praising), or simile (righteousness like great mountains). Each psalm must be read in its own image — not one prophetic code.

scoped_senses.scope_label: Major prophets — mountain of the Lord's house
scoped_senses.sense: The mountain of the house of the Lord exalted above hills — Zion's temple-mount as the magnet for nations in the latter days. Distinct from generic "city" though overlapping: here cultic elevation and teaching from Zion are foregrounded.

Capital or head city: Jerusalem the holy mountain, Babylon/Samaria as mountains, "mountains of Israel" for cities of the land — when peoples/land are politically in view. Newton's Def. 7; Jerusalem and Babylon are the paradigm pair.

"Who are you, O great mountain?" — hostile bulk set before Zerubbabel's work, made a plain. Figure of impeding power in a restoration vision, not a city-name here (though Newton groups it with city-mountain language elsewhere).
Babylon as destroying mountain that ravages the whole earth — city-empire as a volcanic mass to be rolled down and burned. Overlaps city-figure but stresses destructive agency.
Stone cut without hands grows into a great mountain filling the whole earth — kingdom dominion replacing empires. Newton's proportion-changed rule; dominion, not merely a single city.
"Holy mountain of God" applied to Eden-like heights and restored temple topography — sacred elevation in vision, including ideal temple mount.
Ordinary mountain in historical narrative unless the passage reports a dream, sign, or oracle with in-text figurative interpretation.

canon_senses.sense: Smoking oven and flaming torch passing between sacrificial pieces in Abram's vision — theophanic emblems of God's binding oath in the covenant-cutting rite.

canon_senses.label: Theophanic smoke & fire (cross-corpus echo)
canon_senses.sense: Smoke, oven, and furnace language elsewhere may signal divine presence, judgment, or refining — but each passage must be read in its own genre; only Genesis 15 fixes the covenant-passage figure by name.
canon_senses.contrasts_with: Do not back-propagate Daniel/Revelation furnace imagery into Abram's rite, or vice versa, without explicit literary link.

genre_senses.sense: Fire-smoke presence moving through the pieces at sunset in Gen 15:17 — God's sign in Abram's covenant ritual (Hebrew H3536/H8574; Aramaic H861 in Daniel is a distinct lexeme).
If the Aramaic atun (H861) appears in Daniel, treat as that book's court-vision idiom — not Genesis covenant ritual unless the narrative explicitly recalls Abram.
genre_senses.contrasts_with: Lexeme and genre differ from Hebrew furnace in Genesis; scope by book.

entries.label: Man
entries.sense: A man — a male person (ish), a mortal (enosh), or men as people where the sentence uses the word. The ordinary human figure any hearer knows from daily life.

canon_senses.label: Human person (default)
canon_senses.sense: A man — male person (ish), husband, mortal (enosh), or men as people where the sentence uses the word. The ordinary figure any hearer knows.
canon_senses.contrasts_with: Do not read every "man" as a coded realm; default is the human person in the story, law, or prayer unless the passage identifies another sense.

canon_senses.label: Divine anthropomorphism
canon_senses.sense: When the Lord's hand, face, arm, finger, or eyes are named — power, presence, favor, or judgment attributed in terms a man would recognize.
canon_senses.contrasts_with: Distinct from members on a beast in a court vision; cite the passage that names the Lord's member (e.g. Exod 6:6, Isa 51:16).

canon_senses.label: Vision — members on beast or figure
canon_senses.sense: When Daniel or Revelation names eyes, mouths, heads, feet, or a body on a horn or beast, read the members from the vision and its interpretation (Dan 7; Rev 13; Rev 17).
canon_senses.contrasts_with: Not the same as a man's literal hand or face in narrative; the text must name the member on the visionary figure.

genre_senses.sense: Man as patriarch, husband, worker, or mortal in narrative and law; the Lord's hand and arm at the exodus; Adam and his members in the garden account (rib, side, heel).
